content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 27, 1988


Honorable George C. Hardie
Mayor, City of Cathedral City
68-625 Perez Road
Cathedral City, CA  92234

                                    Re:  Your Request For Advice
                                         Our File No. A-88-360

Dear Mayor Hardie:

     You requested advice about application of conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/
to your duties on the City Council of the City of Cathedral
City.

     Over the telephone on October 24, 1988, you told me that
facts in your letter have changed.  The city council will now
be considering placement on the ballot of a measure authorizing
card clubs.  Instead, you as a private citizen will be
sponsoring a petition campaign to put the measure on the
ballot.  Consequently, part of your advice request now is
moot.

     Nevertheless, you still need clarification about
application of Government Code Section 1090 to a city council
decision about a card club license application.  The
Commission, however, does not administer this section.  We
refer you to your city attorney or to the Office of the
Attorney General for advice regarding Section 1090.

_________________________________________________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Honorable George C. Hardie
October 27, 1988
Page -2-


I am sorry it took so long to respond to your advice
request. Please call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have any
questions about this letter.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

[illegible handwritten signature]

By: Margarita Altamirano
Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:MA:aa

68-625 PEREZ ROAD • CATHEDRAL CITY • CALIFORNIA 92234
GENERAL ADMINISTRATION 619-324-8388
COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT 619-321-1531

George G. Hardie
Mayor

September 12, 1988

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Gentlemen:

I am the mayor of Cathedral City, California. I was elected as a
councilman in April, 1988, and elected by my peers to the office
of mayor for a one year term.

In January of 1987, as a businessman, I made a proposal to the
City of Cathedral City regarding the establishment of a card club
and hotel operation on property that I own in the City at 69-375
Ramon Drive. This property currently houses a small hotel. My
proposal was rejected by several members of the council who were
opposed to gambling. At that time the issue was dropped.

Recently, this issue has surfaced with what appears to be
substantial public support and, therefore, my questions are:

1. Can I, as mayor and a potential license applicant, vote
to place the issue before the voters? As you know, the
question for a card club is asked simply, 'Shall there
be card clubs in the city?'

2. What input, if any, may I as mayor and as a potential
license applicant, have in the drafting of an enabling
ordinance? I believe it is the intent to place a
full draft of the ordinance also before the voters.

3. As a private citizen with my own funds, I assume that I
am accorded the same rights as other citizens and may
actively and financially support the passage of the
ordinance.

68-625 PEREZ ROAD • CATHEDRAL CITY • CALIFORNIA 92234
GENERAL ADMINISTRATION 619/324-8388
COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT 619/321-1531

George G. Hardie
Mayor

September 12, 1988
Page 2


4.    I would also need a clarification on 1090 (?) of the
      Government Code. It is obviously self-evident that I
      can have no involvement in the license approval
      process, but would this license to operate a card club
      be considered a "contract" ?

      If it is deemed to be a contract, would I have to
      resign as mayor if I made application for the license?
      At what point would I resign -- when the license
      application is made or when it was granted?

5.    Is there such a thing as a "leave of absence"? If I am
      rejected as the licensee, could I then resume my
      mayoralty duties?

6.    If I am compelled to resign and am the successful
      applicant, at some point in the future, may I run for
      public office in this city again?

7.    If this is placed before the voters by a petition drive
      that I sponsor, outside of the council directing it to
      the ballot, would this affect any of the above
      parameters?

This issue is now here and time is of the essence. I know you
get many requests for rulings, but I would appreciate a response
on this as soon as possible.

Sincerely yours,

George G. Hardie
Mayor

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 19, 1988


Honorable George G. Hardie
Mayor, City of Cathedral City
68-625 Perez Road
Cathedral City, CA 92234

Re:  88-360

Dear Mayor Hardie:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on September 14, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Margarita Altamirano, an
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,


Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

DMG:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660
